item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fellow prosopagnosia sufferer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's kinda like a MalO, my mental image ended up the same for both of them. Even though MalO had a picture of something different than my mental image.

They're both imaginary friends who won't go away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the general concept- "imaginary friend who doesn't want to be forgotten" meets "inability to distinguish unfamiliar people"- and I really enjoyed the story. There are some great beats in this article, like realizing what was behind the [illegible] box in the second interview, or piecing together the fact that the Foundation isn't just failing to help Kevin but sabotaging him in a big (yet perfectly in-character) way by cutting him off from the people who don't trigger his anomaly.

+ But the more I think about it, the more I feel like it doesn't make sense- and I'm not referring to the anomalous stuff.
- Susana's motivation doesn't match her actions.

Susana needs Kevin to want her around, so she makes other people frightening to Kevin in order to make Kevin dependent on her. That makes sense… except that Susana only interacts with Kevin through other people. So if her strategy works, and he avoids other people entirely, she won't be able to interact with him at all, and she'll end up in the situation she's trying to avoid.

Alternatively, we could assume that Susana can appear to Kevin directly, without piggybacking on the appearance of someone else. But if that's the case, why isn't there any indication that she's tried it? The humanoid-shape-made-of-maggots is an excellent deterrent, and Kevin's behavior indicates that it's working. He desperately wants to avoid dealing with that thing… so if she wants him to like her, why is she wearing it all the time?

I can think of a few things I would try in Susana's position- presenting myself as a friendly face and only other people as terrifying; presenting myself as friendly but only appearing via total strangers who aren't likely to interact on their own; even trying to appear in some other medium entirely (inanimate objects, images of people instead of people themselves).

I think there's a way to tell the story you're aiming for here, where everyone is doing their best but there's no simple solution that makes everyone happy- but this version isn't quite hitting it.

For instance: what if Susana isn't the only one in control here? What if she only appears as the maggot-monster because that's how Kevin thinks of her now, or because that limitation is some side effect of being abandoned and coming back? What if she only uses other people as "platforms" because he doesn't believe in her enough to make her visible on her own- or because he doesn't want to give her the ability to manifest whenever she wants?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personnel delivering food or fluid to SCP-2552 are not to interact with it.

Perhaps replace "food or fluid" with "food and/or fluid." Only if personnel deliver only one at a given time. Fluid also sounds kind of off. Maybe it's just me. I thoroughly enjoyed everything else, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Humanoid shape made of maggots, but to answer your question from what I got:

Susana doesn't want to go back to Abandoned Imaginary Friend Limbo™ because it isn't a pleasant place. She makes everyone Kevin sees look frightening so that he will avoid other people and thus be forced to talk to her because he will have no one else. I would guess that Kevin gradually became accustomed to her early efforts, so she is going to further lengths to try to make him isolate himself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I was little, I would imagine that I could see little monsters and cartoon devil/imps popping out from behind things (on purpose, I don't remember the reason, if there was one). Now I'm almost 30, and I still see things that aren't there (not on purpose), and my darker, post-childhood imagination can get…creative. The maggot-thing would be small change for me.

So I can relate to this, and am horrified at the idea of the things that I see having personalities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The notes section of the first log could use some cleaning up. The first sentence should say in significant detail. The verb tense also switches from past to future. Still upvoted though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno about this one. The whole of me really loves the concept and how it's written, but some deep primal part of me dislikes it for being too mushy. Dr. Lin seems far too open for a Foundation scientist. Even Susanna doesn't cast off an air of evil. There's nothing really malevolent on mischievous about this entity, it's just a horrible, horrible situation. It pains me, but neutral vote for now.

Although I do applaud you for having made me pair a section of sentient air and an antisocial human being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a good one, It really creeped me out. In a good way.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just one question.

Why was it a maggot? Did that place turn it into that or did the boy just imagine him as one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this one. For some reason it just seems really chilling - the randomness of it happening to this poor kid, out of all the ones that have imaginary friends. Knowing, as the reader, how homesick Kevin is; and that is more than probable he will never see any of his family or friends again.

Brrrrrr…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Notice me Kevin-senpaii ;~;
In all seriousness, this article is very nice. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Leme feex dat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solid concept. The idea is nicely unsettling, without trying too hard. I also liked that the entity causing this is acting from totally rational impulses. And while I can appreciate a good monster, it's nice to see things with something driving them other than the urge to hide under beds and go "booogedy-boooo". Upvoted.

One nitpick: When the description characterizes the subject as "claiming to be called Kevin Sanders", it implies that his claims are somehow suspect. I get the whole "skips aren't referred to by name" thing and support it, but it might be less awkward to say that he's "known as Kevin Sanders" or something, since it would be pretty easy for the Foundation to verify that he's known in legal documents and in society as Kevin Sanders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Soulless, you create the most lovely skips. +1, slated for translation to the Spanish wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On review, I have upvoted this article for being Labeouf but better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good. The imaginary friend that doesn't won't to be forgotten. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about the sentiment in that interview log gave me chills. This is well-tread ground for you Soulless, but well executed nonetheless. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really reminds me of the tulpa creppypasta, and I have read other stories about imaginary friends gone wrong; it isn't really original enough for an upvote, for me. I do like the execution, though the description is pretty repetitive (you use request repeatedly) and formatted into choppy short sentences. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, totally soulless' signature (wich in this case I imagine as bloody maggots crawling and becoming flies that explode into further maggots). I kinda liked the diea of the Foundation interviewing an imaginary friend, and Susanna's remembers me of timmy turner's imaginary friend in a way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe don't mess with the punctuation, just the words? People tend to use shorter and less complex words when nervous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it's been mentioned that the interview logs make the kid sound really rushed/anxious. That as kinda what I was going for, but the other problem is that I don't actually know how people talk in certain situations and so I have to basically create a facsimile of how I think people talk. Given that I'm not exactly the most social person, this is usually hella inaccurate.

Could you suggest some things I can do to change things and have him appear less obviously-the-authors-error rushed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You might want to touch up some of the grammar in the interview logs. Sentences like

Maybe when I was 6 or 7 I had an imaginary friend I called her Susana.

Or perhaps it's deliberate, to imply a sense of rushed speech or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it is of any consolation I really hate the viewer too and I'm now horrified at the similarities they have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is basically just Labeouf Viewer, only far less silly, which makes me want to downvote it…

Except I never liked Labeouf Viewer. I am so confused right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I originally made this article because I noticed that I had trouble distinguishing strangers apart. My creative mind somehow turned it to this.

The Whole Explanation
click to hide

Susana is an imaginary friend who was abandoned by Kevin a long time ago. Susana went to the place where imaginary friends go after the reason they exist reject them. It is a terrible place of desperation and despair and longing. Susana came back to Kevin, and while she still cares for him and wants to go back to those childhood days where they both played together, she knows this is impossible. At the same time, if Susana doesn't do anything, she'll be sent back to the terrible place of abandonment.

So Susana did this, and now both Susana and Kevin are effectively trapped in a situation where they are both miserable and there isn't a solution to benefit all parties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read it and was all like, 'Dis is a Soulless article,' and it was.
